section.title: Against the Mountains of Israel
section.sectionRationale: The first spoken oracle of the book turns not to the city but to the land itself, the mountains, hills, ravines, and valleys where Israel built her high places. God sets His face against the very hilltop shrines the people trusted, promising to strew them with the corpses of their own worshipers, yet He also promises to leave a remnant who will remember Him and loathe their idolatry.
section.thesis: God will demolish every high place where Israel exchanged His worship for idols, executing judgment on the land itself, yet even this devastation is aimed at a remnant who will at last know that He is the LORD and turn from the idols that ruined them.
section.frameNote: Three movements: the oracle against the mountains and their high places; the slain lying among their own idols, and the survivors scattered to the nations who will remember and loathe themselves; and the summary word, the land made desolate until all know that the LORD has spoken.

units.label: Set your face against the mountains

verses.text: And the word of the LORD came to me, saying,
“Son of man, set your face against the mountains of Israel and prophesy against them.

You are to say: ‘O mountains of Israel, hear the word of the Lord GOD! This is what the Lord GOD says to the mountains and hills, to the ravines and valleys: I am about to bring a sword against you, and I will destroy your {{bamot|high places}}.
Your altars will be demolished and your incense altars will be smashed; and I will cast down your slain before your idols.
I will lay the corpses of the Israelites before their idols and scatter your bones around your altars.

Wherever you live, the cities will be laid waste and the high places will be demolished, so that your altars will be laid waste and desecrated, your idols smashed and obliterated, your incense altars cut down, and your works blotted out.
The slain will fall among you, and {{yada_yhwh|you will know that I am the LORD}}.

units.label: A remnant who will remember and loathe themselves
units.note: scattered among the nations, yet not forgotten

verses.text: Yet I will leave a remnant, for some of you will escape the sword when you are scattered among the nations and throughout the lands.

Then in the nations to which they have been carried captive, your survivors will remember Me, how I have been grieved by their adulterous hearts that turned away from Me, and by their eyes that lusted after idols. So they will loathe themselves for the evil they have done and for all their abominations.
And they will know that I am the LORD; I did not declare in vain that I would bring this calamity upon them.

units.label: The land made desolate until all know the LORD
units.note: clapping in mourning, then knowledge of God

verses.text: This is what the Lord GOD says: Clap your hands, stomp your feet, and cry out “Alas!” because of all the wicked abominations of the house of Israel, who will fall by sword and famine and plague.
He who is far off will die by the plague, he who is near will fall by the sword, and he who remains will die by famine. So I will vent My fury upon them.

Then you will know that I am the LORD, when their slain lie among their idols around their altars, on every high hill, on all the mountaintops, and under every green tree and leafy oak, the places where they offered fragrant incense to all their idols.
I will stretch out My hand against them, and wherever they live I will make the land a desolate waste, from the wilderness to Diblah. Then they will know that I am the LORD.’”

bamot.gloss: High places, elevated shrines on hills and mountaintops used for idolatrous worship, often under sacred trees. Though sometimes tolerated even by faithful kings, they became so associated with Baal worship and syncretism that the prophets condemn them outright; here God vows to demolish them along with their worshipers.

yada_yhwh.gloss: You will know that I am the LORD, a refrain repeated four times in this short chapter. Judgment itself is meant to produce true knowledge of God, not mere information but a recognition of His reality and holiness that idols could never give.

basic.context: For the first time in the book, Ezekiel delivers a spoken oracle rather than only enacting a sign. He turns to face the mountains and hills of Israel, the very landscape where the people built their high places and offered incense to idols under every green tree. God promises to demolish those shrines and to lay the corpses of their worshipers right there among the idols they trusted, so that the land itself will bear witness to what idolatry actually cost.
basic.hermeneutics: This is poetic prophetic judgment speech addressed personified to the land, a common device that intensifies the message without changing its real target, the people who built and used the high places. The chapter should be read for its theological center, that idolatry brings real judgment yet God preserves a remnant who will come to true repentance, rather than mined for geographic detail about which specific hills are meant.
basic.originalLanguages: The oracle targets the bamot, the high places, hilltop shrines that had become synonymous with idol worship in Israel's history. And four times the chapter repeats the refrain yada YHWH, you will know that I am the LORD, showing that the purpose of judgment is not mere destruction but true, undeniable knowledge of God.
basic.typology: The demolished high places anticipate the New Testament's insistence that no place of false worship can stand before the true and living God, and the promised remnant who remember and loathe their sin anticipates the deeper, Spirit-wrought repentance and self-loathing that leads sinners to Christ.
basic.christ: The refrain, that you will know that I am the LORD, finds its fullest answer in Jesus, in whom the invisible God is made fully known, John 1:18, and the loathing self-recognition promised to the exiles anticipates the godly grief that, Paul says, produces repentance leading to salvation, 2 Corinthians 7:10, a grace made possible only through Christ's atoning work.
basic.god: God is a jealous, holy God who will not share His worship with idols, so much so that He turns His judgment against the very hills and shrines that seemed most secure. Yet even in pronouncing total devastation, He deliberately preserves a remnant, showing that His purpose in judgment is ultimately restorative, that His people might truly know Him.
basic.crossReferences: Leviticus 26:30 already threatened that God would cast Israel's corpses on the corpses of their idols, now fulfilled here. Deuteronomy 12:2-3 commanded destroying every high place of Canaanite worship, a command Israel herself came to need. 2 Corinthians 7:10 speaks of godly sorrow leading to repentance, echoing the remnant's loathing here.
basic.oneStory: This is the long-threatened covenant curse finally landing on the very high places where Israel traded her God for idols, yet even in judgment God's purpose is to bring a remnant to true self-knowledge and true knowledge of Him, a pattern of judgment-that-saves fulfilled fully in Christ, who both bears judgment and brings repentance.

context.literary: The first fully spoken oracle in the book, following three chapters dominated by vision and enacted sign; it establishes the classic Ezekiel oracle pattern of accusation, announced judgment, and the recognition formula, 'then they will know that I am the LORD,' repeated here four times.
context.historical: High places, hilltop and grove shrines, had plagued Israel's worship since the days of the judges and kings, condemned repeatedly yet never fully removed even under reforming kings like Hezekiah and Josiah. By Ezekiel's day this entrenched idolatry, alongside covenant unfaithfulness generally, brings the curses of Leviticus 26 to their fulfillment.
context.canonical: This oracle stands in a long tradition of prophetic denunciation of the high places, from the Deuteronomic law codes through Kings and Chronicles, and it anticipates the New Testament theme that all false worship must ultimately give way to the true knowledge of God revealed in Christ.

hermeneutics.authorIntent: To announce that idolatrous worship centered on the high places would bring real, physical judgment on the land and its people, while also revealing that God's ultimate purpose, even in devastating judgment, is a remnant who comes to true repentance and knowledge of Him.
hermeneutics.descriptionVsPrescription: The passage describes a specific historical judgment on Israel's high places and their worshipers; it does not prescribe that every hilltop or grove is inherently sinful today. Its abiding, prescriptive truth is that God tolerates no rivals in worship and that judgment is meant to produce genuine repentance, not despair.

debates.question: Why does God repeat 'you will know that I am the LORD' so often in this short oracle?

views.label: Judgment itself as revelation
views.case: The refrain marks judgment as a form of self-revelation: God's holiness and reality become undeniable precisely through the severity of what befalls those who denied Him by worshiping idols.

views.label: Mercy embedded even in judgment's purpose
views.case: The same refrain appears connected to the remnant who survive and remember, suggesting the ultimate aim of even the harshest judgment is that people come to true, saving knowledge of God rather than destruction for its own sake.

debates.bearing: Both readings agree the refrain reveals God's central concern throughout the oracle: that people truly know Him, whether that knowledge comes through experiencing judgment or through the mercy of surviving it to repent.

study.originalLanguages: The oracle is aimed squarely at the bamot, the high places that had corrupted Israel's worship for centuries, and it drives home its point with the refrain yada YHWH, you will know that I am the LORD, repeated four times in fourteen verses. Judgment and knowledge of God are bound tightly together throughout: the very devastation of the high places is meant to teach what the high places themselves could never teach, who the LORD truly is.

typology.type: The demolished high places and shattered idols
typology.fulfillment: Anticipates the total exposure of every false object of worship before the true and living God, culminating in the final judgment of every idol in Revelation 9:20.

typology.type: The remnant who remember and loathe themselves for their sin
typology.fulfillment: A picture of the godly sorrow that leads to repentance, 2 Corinthians 7:10, made possible in full only through the Spirit's work applying Christ's finished redemption.

study.christ: The refrain that closes each movement of this oracle, that they will know that I am the LORD, finds its ultimate fulfillment in Jesus Christ, of whom John writes, 'No one has ever seen God, but the one and only Son, who is Himself God and is at the Father's side, He has made Him known,' John 1:18. What idolatry could never deliver, true knowledge of God, is given fully and finally in Christ. And the self-loathing repentance promised to the surviving remnant anticipates the very grace the gospel produces: not mere regret, but a godly sorrow that leads through Christ to life rather than death.
study.god: God is holy and jealous for exclusive worship, unwilling to let entrenched idolatry stand even on the mountains His people considered safest. His judgment is thorough, reaching every hilltop shrine and green tree, yet it is not indiscriminate destruction for its own sake: He deliberately spares a remnant, aiming even His fiercest anger toward the ultimate goal that people would truly know Him.

crossReferences.note: God had long warned He would cast Israel's corpses upon the corpses of their idols, exactly what this oracle now enacts.
The command to destroy every high place of Canaanite worship, a task Israel herself failed to complete and later needed applied to her own idolatry.
Godly sorrow that produces repentance without regret, echoing the loathing self-recognition promised to the remnant.
The final judgment on those who still refuse to abandon idols, the ultimate answer to this oracle's warning.

study.oneStory: This is the long-delayed covenant curse against idolatry finally landing on the hills and shrines Israel trusted more than her God, yet even here God's purpose is not bare destruction: a remnant survives to remember, to loathe their idolatry, and to know Him truly. That pattern, judgment that opens the door to real repentance, reaches its fullest expression in Jesus Christ, through whom alone sinners come to loathe their sin rightly and know the true and living God.

quiz.q: What does God command Ezekiel to prophesy against in this chapter?

options.text: The king of Babylon and his armies.
options.note: The oracle targets Israel's own high places, not Babylon's king.

options.text: The mountains, hills, ravines, and valleys of Israel, and the high places built on them.
options.note: Ezekiel 6:2-3 addresses the land and its idolatrous shrines directly.

options.text: The temple in Jerusalem specifically.
options.note: The focus here is the countryside high places, not the temple building.

options.text: Nothing in particular; the oracle has no clear target.
options.note: The mountains and their idolatrous shrines are named explicitly.

quiz.q: What will happen to the corpses of the slain according to this oracle?

options.text: They will be carried to Babylon for burial.
options.note: The bodies are pictured lying at the high places themselves.

options.text: There will be no slain at all.
options.note: The chapter emphasizes widespread slaughter among the idols.

options.text: They will fall and lie before the very idols the people worshiped.
options.note: Ezekiel 6:4-5 and 6:13 describe corpses lying among their idols and altars.

options.text: They will be given honorable burial in Jerusalem.
options.note: The oracle pictures dishonorable exposure, not honorable burial.

quiz.q: What promise does God make even within this harsh judgment oracle?

options.text: He promises that no one will survive at all.
options.note: The opposite; a remnant is explicitly preserved.

options.text: He promises immediate restoration of the temple.
options.note: No such immediate restoration is promised in this chapter.

options.text: He makes no promise of any kind.
options.note: The remnant promise is stated clearly in verses 8-9.

options.text: He will leave a remnant who escape and are scattered among the nations.
options.note: Ezekiel 6:8-9 promises survivors who will remember and loathe their sin.

quiz.q: What will the surviving remnant come to feel about their past idolatry?

options.text: They will loathe themselves for their evil and abominations.
options.note: Ezekiel 6:9 describes this self-loathing response to remembering God.

options.text: They will feel nothing at all.
options.note: A strong emotional and moral response is described.

options.text: They will feel proud of their former worship.
options.note: The text describes loathing and grief, not pride.

options.text: They will blame God entirely for their circumstances.
options.note: The loathing is directed at their own evil and abominations, not at God.

quiz.q: What refrain is repeated throughout this chapter to explain the purpose of judgment?

options.text: Then they will forget that I am the LORD.
options.note: The refrain is about coming to know God, the opposite of forgetting.

options.text: Then the nations will rejoice.
options.note: No such refrain about the nations rejoicing appears here.

options.text: Then they will know that I am the LORD.
options.note: This exact refrain occurs four times across the chapter.

options.text: No refrain is repeated in this chapter.
options.note: The knowledge-of-the-LORD refrain is a defining feature of the passage.

questions: The high places felt safe and familiar to Israel, yet they were exactly what God judged. What familiar, comfortable habits or attachments in your own life might be quietly competing with true worship of God?
God repeats 'then you will know that I am the LORD' four times in this short chapter. Where in your own story has hardship or loss led you to know God more truly, not just know facts about Him?
The remnant's repentance began with remembering God and loathing their own sin, not with excusing it. How comfortable are you with genuine, specific sorrow over sin rather than vague, general guilt?
God's judgment on the high places was thorough, reaching every hill and green tree. Is there an area of compromise in your life you have been unwilling to let God thoroughly address?

Even in devastating judgment, God's purpose was ultimately a remnant restored to true knowledge of Him. How does trusting God's good purposes change the way you endure a season of discipline or hardship?

translationNotes.note: The high places, translated bamot in Hebrew, were elevated open-air shrines, often on hilltops or under sacred trees, associated throughout Israel's history with syncretistic and idolatrous worship.
The phrase describing adulterous hearts and lusting eyes uses covenant marriage imagery common in the prophets, picturing idolatry as spiritual unfaithfulness to a husband, here God Himself.

Diblah, marking the northern extent of the desolation, is generally understood to be a scribal or textual variant for Riblah, a location further north in Syria used as a staging point by Babylonian forces.

meta.notes: BSB text fetched this session via the makor scripture toolkit for Ezekiel 6:1-14. An em dash in verse 9 was replaced with a comma; no other line breaks or em dashes were present. No sermon from the trusted cluster (Begg, Keller, Piper, Ferguson, Carson) was found specifically scoped to Ezekiel 6 alone, so sermon status is none.
